([/ˈsɛrə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SERR-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Italian: [[ˈtʃeːra]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born June 7, 1988)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a Canadian actor and musician. **Michael Austin Cera** ([/ˈsɛrə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SERR-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Italian: [[ˈtʃeːra]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born June 7, 1988)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a Canadian actor and musician. Over his career he has received nominations for a [British Academy Film Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"), three [Critics' Choice Movie Awards](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Movie_Awards "Critics' Choice Movie Awards"), four [Screen Actors Guild Awards](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Awards "Screen Actors Guild Awards"), and a [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Award "Tony Award").

Cera became known for portraying leading roles in a string of comedic films such as *[Superbad](/wiki/Superbad_(film) "Superbad (film)")* (2007), *[Juno](/wiki/Juno_(film) "Juno (film)")* (2007), *[Scott Pilgrim vs. the World](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim_vs._the_World "Scott Pilgrim vs. the World")* (2010), *[Nick & Norah's Infinite Playlist](/wiki/Nick_%26_Norah%27s_Infinite_Playlist "Nick & Norah's Infinite Playlist")* (2008), and *[Youth in Revolt](/wiki/Youth_in_Revolt_(film) "Youth in Revolt (film)")* (2009). He took supporting roles in both comedies and dramas including *[Confessions of a Dangerous Mind](/wiki/Confessions_of_a_Dangerous_Mind_(film) "Confessions of a Dangerous Mind (film)")* (2002), *[This Is the End](/wiki/This_Is_the_End "This Is the End")* (2013), *[Molly's Game](/wiki/Molly%27s_Game "Molly's Game")* (2017), *[Person to Person](/wiki/Person_to_Person_(film) "Person to Person (film)")* (2017), *[Gloria Bell](/wiki/Gloria_Bell "Gloria Bell")* (2019), *[Barbie](/wiki/Barbie_(film) "Barbie (film)")* (2023), *[Dream Scenario](/wiki/Dream_Scenario "Dream Scenario")* (2023) and *[The Phoenician Scheme](/wiki/The_Phoenician_Scheme "The Phoenician Scheme")* (2025). He voiced [Dick Grayson / Robin](/wiki/Dick_Grayson "Dick Grayson") in *[The Lego Batman Movie](/wiki/The_Lego_Batman_Movie "The Lego Batman Movie")* (2017).

Cera gained prominence portraying [George Michael Bluth](/wiki/List_of_Arrested_Development_characters "List of Arrested Development characters") in the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") [sitcom](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom") *[Arrested Development](/wiki/Arrested_Development "Arrested Development")* from 2003 to 2006 and then again from 2013 to 2019. He took a leading role in the [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") comedy series *[Life & Beth](/wiki/Life_%26_Beth "Life & Beth")* (2022–2024), and voiced roles including Brother Bear in the [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") children's series *[The Berenstain Bears](/wiki/The_Berenstain_Bears_(2003_TV_series) "The Berenstain Bears (2003 TV series)")*, Hank in *[Paws of Fury: The Legend of Hank](/wiki/Paws_of_Fury:_The_Legend_of_Hank "Paws of Fury: The Legend of Hank")* (2022) and reprising his roles as [Scott Pilgrim](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim_(character) "Scott Pilgrim (character)") in the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") series *[Scott Pilgrim Takes Off](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim_Takes_Off "Scott Pilgrim Takes Off")* (2023) and Barry, a sausage in *[Sausage Party](/wiki/Sausage_Party "Sausage Party")* (2016) and its [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video") sequel series *[Sausage Party: Foodtopia](/wiki/Sausage_Party:_Foodtopia "Sausage Party: Foodtopia")* (2024).

Cera is also known for his [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(theatre) "Broadway (theatre)") performances in the [Kenneth Lonergan](/wiki/Kenneth_Lonergan "Kenneth Lonergan") plays *[This Is Our Youth](/wiki/This_Is_Our_Youth "This Is Our Youth")* in 2014, *[Lobby Hero](/wiki/Lobby_Hero "Lobby Hero")* in 2018, for which he received a nomination for the [Tony Award for Best Featured Actor in a Play](/wiki/Tony_Award_for_Best_Performance_by_a_Featured_Actor_in_a_Play "Tony Award for Best Performance by a Featured Actor in a Play"), and *[The Waverly Gallery](/wiki/The_Waverly_Gallery "The Waverly Gallery")* in 2019. In addition to acting, Cera is a musician, having released his debut album *[True That](/wiki/True_That "True That")* in 2014. Cera has also performed as the touring bassist for indie rock supergroup [Mister Heavenly](/wiki/Mister_Heavenly "Mister Heavenly").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Cera was born on June 7, 1988, in [Brampton](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton"), Ontario. He is the son of Linda (née Cockman) and Luigi Cera, a technician. He is of [Sicilian](/wiki/Italian_Canadians "Italian Canadians") descent through his father.[[2]](#cite_note-2) His parents both worked for [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox").[[3]](#cite_note-Biography_Today-3) Cera has an older sister, Jordan, and a younger sister, Molly. He became interested in acting after viewing *[Ghostbusters](/wiki/Ghostbusters "Ghostbusters")* repeatedly when sick with the [chicken pox](/wiki/Chicken_pox "Chicken pox") at the age of three.[[4]](#cite_note-WTF_with_Marc_Maron,_Ep._265-4) Cera memorized all the dialogue and idolized [Bill Murray](/wiki/Bill_Murray "Bill Murray").[[5]](#cite_note-Biography_Today,_pp.20-5) He enrolled in [The Second City](/wiki/The_Second_City "The Second City"), [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), and took improvisation classes.[[5]](#cite_note-Biography_Today,_pp.20-5)

Cera attended Conestoga Public School, Robert H. Lagerquist Senior Public School, and [Heart Lake Secondary School](/wiki/Heart_Lake_Secondary_School "Heart Lake Secondary School") until grade nine. After starting acting, he completed school online through grade twelve.[[6]](#cite_note-Luigi-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Strauss-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1999–2008: Child acting and breakthrough

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1999–2008: Child acting and breakthrough")]

His first role was an unpaid appearance in a [Tim Hortons](/wiki/Tim_Hortons "Tim Hortons") summer camp commercial.[[6]](#cite_note-Luigi-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Strauss-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) That appearance eventually landed him a position in a Pillsbury commercial, in which he poked the [Pillsbury Doughboy](/wiki/Pillsbury_Doughboy "Pillsbury Doughboy") and had his first role with lines.[[9]](#cite_note-Jones-9)

In 1999, Cera was cast as Larrabe Hicks in the Canadian children's show *[I Was a Sixth Grade Alien](/wiki/I_Was_a_Sixth_Grade_Alien "I Was a Sixth Grade Alien")*, which ran for two seasons.[[10]](#cite_note-10) That year, he also appeared in the television films *[What Katy Did](/wiki/What_Katy_Did "What Katy Did")* and *[Switching Goals](/wiki/Switching_Goals "Switching Goals")*, starring the [Olsen twins](/wiki/Mary-Kate_and_Ashley_Olsen "Mary-Kate and Ashley Olsen").[[11]](#cite_note-ScreenCrush-11)

The next year Cera made his theatrical film debut in the science fiction film *[Frequency](/wiki/Frequency_(2000_film) "Frequency (2000 film)")* (2000) as the son of [Noah Emmerich](/wiki/Noah_Emmerich "Noah Emmerich")'s character.[[11]](#cite_note-ScreenCrush-11) Cera also appeared in the films *[Steal This Movie!](/wiki/Steal_This_Movie! "Steal This Movie!")* and *Ultimate G's: Zac's Flying Dream* in 2000. He had his first leading role in the latter film, which was presented in [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") theaters.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Biography.com-13) Cera appeared in several television films in 2001, including *[My Louisiana Sky](/wiki/My_Louisiana_Sky "My Louisiana Sky")* and *[The Familiar Stranger](/wiki/The_Familiar_Stranger "The Familiar Stranger")*. He also began voicing Josh Spitz in the animated series *[Braceface](/wiki/Braceface "Braceface")*, which he continued until 2004.[[11]](#cite_note-ScreenCrush-11)

In 2002, Cera played the young [Chuck Barris](/wiki/Chuck_Barris "Chuck Barris") (played by [Sam Rockwell](/wiki/Sam_Rockwell "Sam Rockwell")) in the [George Clooney](/wiki/George_Clooney "George Clooney")-directed film *[Confessions of a Dangerous Mind](/wiki/Confessions_of_a_Dangerous_Mind_(film) "Confessions of a Dangerous Mind (film)")*.[[14]](#cite_note-14) He provided the voice for Brother Bear – an [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphic "Anthropomorphic") [bear](/wiki/Bear "Bear") – in the 2003 *[The Berenstain Bears](/wiki/The_Berenstain_Bears_(2003_TV_series) "The Berenstain Bears (2003 TV series)")* animated series, which aired for three seasons.[[13]](#cite_note-Biography.com-13)

> "*Arrested Development* never felt safe. Even the first season, we did thirteen episodes, and we thought we'd never do a back nine. So I never thought in a million years we'd get to make three seasons. I was happy we got that far. I thought it was really good, and I'm really proud of it. I don't think we made a bad episode."

—Michael Cera, *[Esquire](/wiki/Esquire_(magazine) "Esquire (magazine)")* (2009)[[9]](#cite_note-Jones-9)

He had a role in the critically panned [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") pilot *[The Grubbs](/wiki/The_Grubbs "The Grubbs")* in 2002, which was never aired.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Guardian-16) But Cera successfully auditioned for a part in another Fox sitcom, *[Arrested Development](/wiki/Arrested_Development "Arrested Development")*. This began airing in November 2003 and ran for three seasons.[[16]](#cite_note-Guardian-16) The show follows the formerly wealthy and [dysfunctional](/wiki/Dysfunctional_family "Dysfunctional family") Bluth family, with Cera playing [George Michael Bluth](/wiki/George_Michael_Bluth "George Michael Bluth"), the teenage son of character [Michael Bluth](/wiki/Michael_Bluth "Michael Bluth"), played by [Jason Bateman](/wiki/Jason_Bateman "Jason Bateman").[[17]](#cite_note-17) After three seasons, Fox canceled the series in 2006 due to low viewership, although it had received critical acclaim.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 2006, Cera created and starred in a parody of *[Impossible is Nothing](/wiki/Impossible_Is_Nothing_(video_r%C3%A9sum%C3%A9) "Impossible Is Nothing (video résumé)")*, a video résumé created by Aleksey Vayner.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Cera and his *Arrested Development* co-star [Alia Shawkat](/wiki/Alia_Shawkat "Alia Shawkat") guest-starred as a pair of college students in the teen noir drama *[Veronica Mars](/wiki/Veronica_Mars "Veronica Mars")*, in the episode "[The Rapes of Graff](/wiki/The_Rapes_of_Graff "The Rapes of Graff")" in 2006.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Along with best friend [Clark Duke](/wiki/Clark_Duke "Clark Duke"), Cera wrote and starred in a series of short videos released on their website.[[16]](#cite_note-Guardian-16) Duke originated the idea, as he was enrolled at [Loyola Marymount University](/wiki/Loyola_Marymount_University "Loyola Marymount University") and used their videos for his film school studies.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In 2007, the pair signed a deal with [CBS Television](/wiki/CBS_Television "CBS Television") to write, produce, direct, and act in a short-form comedy series entitled *[Clark and Michael](/wiki/Clark_and_Michael "Clark and Michael")*. The show featured guest stars such as [David Cross](/wiki/David_Cross "David Cross"), [Andy Richter](/wiki/Andy_Richter "Andy Richter") and [Patton Oswalt](/wiki/Patton_Oswalt "Patton Oswalt"), and was distributed via CBS's internet channel, [CBS Innertube](/wiki/CBS_Innertube "CBS Innertube").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Michael_Cera_-_001.jpg/250px-Michael_Cera_-_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Cera_-_001.jpg)

Cera in 2007

In May 2007, Cera appeared in a staged comedy video that shows him being fired from the lead role of the film *[Knocked Up](/wiki/Knocked_Up "Knocked Up")*, after belittling and arguing with its director [Judd Apatow](/wiki/Judd_Apatow "Judd Apatow"), in a scene that mocks the [David O. Russell](/wiki/David_O._Russell "David O. Russell") blow up on the set of *[I Heart Huckabees](/wiki/I_Heart_Huckabees "I Heart Huckabees")*.[[16]](#cite_note-Guardian-16) Cera starred in the Apatow-produced teen comedy *[Superbad](/wiki/Superbad_(film) "Superbad (film)")* alongside [Jonah Hill](/wiki/Jonah_Hill "Jonah Hill"). Their characters in the film – two [virgin](/wiki/Virgin "Virgin") teenagers about to graduate from high school whose party plans go awry – were based on the comedy's writers [Seth Rogen](/wiki/Seth_Rogen "Seth Rogen") and [Evan Goldberg](/wiki/Evan_Goldberg "Evan Goldberg").[[23]](#cite_note-23) *Superbad* was released in cinemas in August 2007, topping the US box office for two weeks in a row.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Cera's performance was critically acclaimed: *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* reviewer said that the film "belongs to Michael Cera" for capturing "teenage sexual abashment as indelibly as he did in the role of George Michael [on *Arrested Development*]."[[25]](#cite_note-25) *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said that he was "excellent" and [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") praised Cera and Hill for playing "off each other beautifully".[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In November 2007, Cera hosted a live, staged version of *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*; it was not broadcast due to the ongoing [2007 Writers Guild of America Strike](/wiki/2007_Writers_Guild_of_America_Strike "2007 Writers Guild of America Strike").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[7]](#cite_note-Strauss-7) In his second film of 2007, Cera co-starred in *[Juno](/wiki/Juno_(film) "Juno (film)")* as Paulie Bleeker, a teenager who has impregnated his long-time school friend Juno (played by [Elliot Page](/wiki/Elliot_Page "Elliot Page")).[[29]](#cite_note-29) For *Superbad* and *Juno*, Cera won Breakthrough Artist in the [Austin Film Critics Association Awards 2007](/wiki/Austin_Film_Critics_Association_Awards_2007 "Austin Film Critics Association Awards 2007"), and was included in *[Entertainment Weekly's](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* "30 Under 30" list in February 2008.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Cera starred alongside [Kat Dennings](/wiki/Kat_Dennings "Kat Dennings") in the romantic comedy-drama *[Nick & Norah's Infinite Playlist](/wiki/Nick_%26_Norah%27s_Infinite_Playlist "Nick & Norah's Infinite Playlist")* (2008), in which they played two strangers who bond over their shared love of a band and try to find their secret show.[[32]](#cite_note-32) He starred in the comedy *[Extreme Movie](/wiki/Extreme_Movie "Extreme Movie")* (2008), which was composed of vignettes focusing on [teen sex](/wiki/Teen_sex "Teen sex").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Cera held a recurring role on the comedy series *[Childrens Hospital](/wiki/Childrens_Hospital "Childrens Hospital")* from 2008 to 2016 as Sal Viscuso, a hospital staffer who is known only by his voice through an [intercom](/wiki/Intercom "Intercom").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### 2009–2013: Varied success and stage debut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2009–2013: Varied success and stage debut")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Michael_Cera_as_Captain_America_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Michael_Cera_as_Captain_America_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Cera_as_Captain_America_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Cera dressed as [Captain America](/wiki/Captain_America "Captain America") to promote *[Scott Pilgrim vs. the World](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim_vs._the_World "Scott Pilgrim vs. the World")* at the 2010 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con")

Cera played a fictionalized version of himself in the independent romantic comedy *[Paper Heart](/wiki/Paper_Heart_(film) "Paper Heart (film)")* (2009). It explored the fictional relationship between Cera and the film's writer [Charlyne Yi](/wiki/Charlyne_Yi "Charlyne Yi"), also playing herself. Cera and Yi composed the film's score together.[[35]](#cite_note-35) That year Cera starred opposite [Jack Black](/wiki/Jack_Black "Jack Black") in the comedy *[Year One](/wiki/Year_One_(film) "Year One (film)")*, set during the [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age"). The film, directed by [Harold Ramis](/wiki/Harold_Ramis "Harold Ramis"), was poorly received, although *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine critic Mary Pols said that Cera's performance saved the film from being a "catastrophe".[[36]](#cite_note-36) In his final film of 2009, Cera starred in *[Youth in Revolt](/wiki/Youth_in_Revolt_(film) "Youth in Revolt (film)")*, an adaptation of the [eponymous novel](/wiki/Youth_in_Revolt "Youth in Revolt"). He played a shy teenager named Nick Twisp who creates a destructive [alter ego](/wiki/Alter_ego "Alter ego"), François Dillinger, after becoming smitten with a girl, played by [Portia Doubleday](/wiki/Portia_Doubleday "Portia Doubleday").[[16]](#cite_note-Guardian-16)[[37]](#cite_note-twisp-37)

Cera had also begun to write. His first published short story, "Pinecone", appeared in *[McSweeney's Quarterly](/wiki/Timothy_McSweeney%27s_Quarterly_Concern#30 "Timothy McSweeney's Quarterly Concern")* thirtieth issue in 2009.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Cera was cast as [Scott Pilgrim](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim "Scott Pilgrim") in the film adaptation of the [graphic novel](/wiki/Graphic_novel "Graphic novel") series by [Bryan Lee O'Malley](/wiki/Bryan_Lee_O%27Malley "Bryan Lee O'Malley"). The film's director, [Edgar Wright](/wiki/Edgar_Wright "Edgar Wright"), had seen his work in *Arrested Development* and believed that Cera was an actor "audiences will still follow even when the character is being a bit of an ass."[[39]](#cite_note-39) The film, *[Scott Pilgrim vs. the World](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim_vs._the_World "Scott Pilgrim vs. the World")*, follows Pilgrim, a musician who must battle the seven evil exes of his girlfriend [Ramona](/wiki/Ramona_Flowers "Ramona Flowers") (played by [Mary Elizabeth Winstead](/wiki/Mary_Elizabeth_Winstead "Mary Elizabeth Winstead")). It was released in cinemas in August 2010. It did poorly at the box office, grossing $47.7 million against a production budget of $85–90 million.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Cera made a guest appearance in "[The Daughter Also Rises](/wiki/The_Daughter_Also_Rises "The Daughter Also Rises")", a 2012 episode of the animated sitcom *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*, as the voice of Nick, a love interest to [Lisa Simpson](/wiki/Lisa_Simpson "Lisa Simpson").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Cera made his theater debut in a production of [Kenneth Lonergan](/wiki/Kenneth_Lonergan "Kenneth Lonergan")'s play *[This Is Our Youth](/wiki/This_Is_Our_Youth "This Is Our Youth")* in a two-week run during March 2012 at the [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House"). The play also featured his *Scott Pilgrim* co-star [Kieran Culkin](/wiki/Kieran_Culkin "Kieran Culkin") and actress [Tavi Gevinson](/wiki/Tavi_Gevinson "Tavi Gevinson").[[43]](#cite_note-43) A [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") production at the [Cort Theater](/wiki/James_Earl_Jones_Theatre "James Earl Jones Theatre") opened in September 2014 and closed in January 2015. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* theater critic [Ben Brantley](/wiki/Ben_Brantley "Ben Brantley") praised Cera for achieving "something remarkable": "the sense of an amorphous being assuming and losing shape in the course of roughly 12 hours".[[44]](#cite_note-44) Also in 2012, Cera played a supporting role in the drama *[The End of Love](/wiki/The_End_of_Love "The End of Love")* and appeared in the short film *The Immigrant*.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

*Arrested Development* was revived for a fourth season in 2012 by [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"), with Cera reprising his role as George Michael Bluth. Cera also worked in the writers' room and served as a consulting producer during its production.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The season was released in May 2013.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Michael_Cera_2012_%28Cropped%29.jpg/250px-Michael_Cera_2012_%28Cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Cera_2012_(Cropped).jpg)

Cera in 2012

Cera collaborated with Chilean filmmaker [Sebastián Silva](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Silva_(director) "Sebastián Silva (director)") on two films in 2013 – *[Magic Magic](/wiki/Magic_Magic_(2013_film) "Magic Magic (2013 film)")* and *[Crystal Fairy & the Magical Cactus](/wiki/Crystal_Fairy_%26_the_Magical_Cactus "Crystal Fairy & the Magical Cactus")* – both of which were filmed in Chile and premiered at the [2013 Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/2013_Sundance_Film_Festival "2013 Sundance Film Festival").[[49]](#cite_note-Magic_Magic_Crystal_Fairy-49) He spent "five hours a day learning Spanish" for *Magic Magic*.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Cera was featured most prominently in *Crystal Fairy*, in which he starred as a self-absorbed man travelling Chile with a woman named "Crystal Fairy" (played by [Gaby Hoffmann](/wiki/Gaby_Hoffmann "Gaby Hoffmann")) while bearing a [cactus](/wiki/Cactus "Cactus").[[49]](#cite_note-Magic_Magic_Crystal_Fairy-49) Along with [Reggie Watts](/wiki/Reggie_Watts "Reggie Watts"), [Tim & Eric](/wiki/Tim_%26_Eric "Tim & Eric"), and [Sarah Silverman](/wiki/Sarah_Silverman "Sarah Silverman"), Cera created the [web-based](/wiki/Web-based "Web-based") comedy [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel [Jash](/wiki/Jash "Jash") in March 2013, where he has posted short films which he directs and/or stars in.[[51]](#cite_note-USA_Today_Introduces_Jash-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Rolling_Stone_Magazine_Introduces_Jash-52) These films include the comedy-drama *[Gregory Go Boom](/wiki/Gregory_Go_Boom "Gregory Go Boom")* (2013), in which Cera played a [paraplegic](/wiki/Hereditary_spastic_paraplegia "Hereditary spastic paraplegia") man, and his directorial debut *Brazzaville Teen-Ager* (2013), co-starring [Charles Grodin](/wiki/Charles_Grodin "Charles Grodin") as his sick father.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) He played an exaggerated version of himself in the apocalyptic comedy film *[This Is the End](/wiki/This_Is_the_End "This Is the End")*, which was released in summer of 2013 and featured his *Superbad* co-stars Jonah Hill and Seth Rogen.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Throughout 2013, Cera also appeared on *[Burning Love](/wiki/Burning_Love_(web_series) "Burning Love (web series)")*, a web spoof of [reality dating](/wiki/Reality_television#Reality_competition_or_game_shows "Reality television") competition shows,[[56]](#cite_note-56) and on an episode of *[Drunk History](/wiki/Drunk_History "Drunk History")* as [John Endecott](/wiki/John_Endecott "John Endecott"). Cera had previously played [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") in a comedic retelling of [Hamilton's duel](/wiki/Burr%E2%80%93Hamilton_duel "Burr–Hamilton duel") with [Aaron Burr](/wiki/Aaron_Burr "Aaron Burr") on the show's first episode as a web series in 2008 before it was adapted into a television show.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### 2014–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2014–present")]

Cera appeared in his *Arrested Development* co-star [David Cross](/wiki/David_Cross "David Cross")' 2014 film *[Hits](/wiki/Hits_(film) "Hits (film)")*, playing a [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana") [dealer](/wiki/Drug_dealer "Drug dealer").[[58]](#cite_note-58) He also co-starred alongside [John Hawkes](/wiki/John_Hawkes_(actor) "John Hawkes (actor)") and [Sally Hawkins](/wiki/Sally_Hawkins "Sally Hawkins") in [Charlie Kaufman](/wiki/Charlie_Kaufman "Charlie Kaufman")'s [television pilot](/wiki/Television_pilot "Television pilot") *How & Why*, which was rejected by [FX](/wiki/FX_(TV_channel) "FX (TV channel)").[[59]](#cite_note-59) After a brief, "menacing" appearance in the drama *[Entertainment](/wiki/Entertainment_(2015_film) "Entertainment (2015 film)")* (2015),[[60]](#cite_note-60) Cera appeared in the [prequel](/wiki/Prequel "Prequel") to the 2001 comedy film *[Wet Hot American Summer](/wiki/Wet_Hot_American_Summer "Wet Hot American Summer")*, the comedy series *[Wet Hot American Summer: First Day of Camp](/wiki/Wet_Hot_American_Summer:_First_Day_of_Camp "Wet Hot American Summer: First Day of Camp")* and in the Christmas musical comedy film *[A Very Murray Christmas](/wiki/A_Very_Murray_Christmas "A Very Murray Christmas")* as [Bill Murray](/wiki/Bill_Murray "Bill Murray")'s fictional talent agent.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Cera then voiced a [hot dog](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog") trying to escape his fate in a supermarket in the animated comedy *[Sausage Party](/wiki/Sausage_Party "Sausage Party")* (2016).[[62]](#cite_note-Chval-62) In 2015, Cera made a cameo on [Louis C.K.](/wiki/Louis_C.K. "Louis C.K.")'s *[Louie](/wiki/Louie_(American_TV_series) "Louie (American TV series)")* on [FX](/wiki/FX_(TV_channel) "FX (TV channel)"), in the season five episode "Sleepover" alongside [Glenn Close](/wiki/Glenn_Close "Glenn Close"), [John Lithgow](/wiki/John_Lithgow "John Lithgow"), and [Matthew Broderick](/wiki/Matthew_Broderick "Matthew Broderick").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Cera had five film releases in 2017, the first of which was the animated superhero comedy *[The Lego Batman Movie](/wiki/The_Lego_Batman_Movie "The Lego Batman Movie")*, in which he voiced the [Batman](/wiki/Batman "Batman")'s sidekick [Robin](/wiki/Robin_(character) "Robin (character)").[[62]](#cite_note-Chval-62) He played a supporting role as a sleazy [car salesman](/wiki/Car_salesman "Car salesman") in the comedy *[How to Be a Latin Lover](/wiki/How_to_Be_a_Latin_Lover "How to Be a Latin Lover")* and co-starred in [Janicza Bravo](/wiki/Janicza_Bravo "Janicza Bravo")'s first full-length feature, the comedy-drama *[Lemon](/wiki/Lemon_(2017_film) "Lemon (2017 film)")*. He played an actor described as having a "wedge of hair that makes him look like [Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin") crossed with *[Eraserhead](/wiki/Eraserhead "Eraserhead")*", by *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* critic [Owen Gleiberman](/wiki/Owen_Gleiberman "Owen Gleiberman").[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) Cera starred opposite [Abbi Jacobson](/wiki/Abbi_Jacobson "Abbi Jacobson") in the drama *[Person to Person](/wiki/Person_to_Person_(film) "Person to Person (film)")*, focusing on the struggles of different people over the course of one day in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). Cera and Jacobson are featured as a pair of crime reporters investigating a possible murder.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In his final film of the year, [Aaron Sorkin](/wiki/Aaron_Sorkin "Aaron Sorkin")'s crime drama *[Molly's Game](/wiki/Molly%27s_Game "Molly's Game")*, Cera played a celebrity known only as Player X who participates in a high-stakes, underground poker empire run by [Molly Bloom](/wiki/Molly_Bloom_(author) "Molly Bloom (author)") (played by [Jessica Chastain](/wiki/Jessica_Chastain "Jessica Chastain")). Cera's fictional character in the film was said to be a [composite character](/wiki/Composite_character "Composite character") of celebrity poker players and actors [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio"), [Tobey Maguire](/wiki/Tobey_Maguire "Tobey Maguire") and [Ben Affleck](/wiki/Ben_Affleck "Ben Affleck").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

A "giant fan" of director [David Lynch](/wiki/David_Lynch "David Lynch"),[[62]](#cite_note-Chval-62) Cera made a guest appearance in the [2017 revival](/wiki/Twin_Peaks_(season_3) "Twin Peaks (season 3)") of Lynch and [Mark Frost](/wiki/Mark_Frost "Mark Frost")'s television show *[Twin Peaks](/wiki/Twin_Peaks "Twin Peaks")* in the show's fourth episode, as Wally "Brando" Brennan, the son of Deputy Sheriff [Andy Brennan](/wiki/List_of_Twin_Peaks_characters#Andy_Brennan "List of Twin Peaks characters") and his wife [Lucy Brennan](/wiki/Lucy_Moran "Lucy Moran").[[68]](#cite_note-WallyBrando-68) The appearance contained several references to the work of actor [Marlon Brando](/wiki/Marlon_Brando "Marlon Brando"): Wally shares the same birthday and is nicknamed after Brando.[[68]](#cite_note-WallyBrando-68) Cera returned to the stage in March 2018, starring in a second Kenneth Lonergan production, *[Lobby Hero](/wiki/Lobby_Hero "Lobby Hero")*, at the [Helen Hayes Theatre](/wiki/Helen_Hayes_Theatre "Helen Hayes Theatre") on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"). The play also stars [Chris Evans](/wiki/Chris_Evans_(actor) "Chris Evans (actor)"), [Brian Tyree Henry](/wiki/Brian_Tyree_Henry "Brian Tyree Henry") and [Bel Powley](/wiki/Bel_Powley "Bel Powley").[[69]](#cite_note-69) Cera and Henry were both nominated for [Best Featured Actor in a Play](/wiki/Tony_Award_for_Best_Featured_Actor_in_a_Play "Tony Award for Best Featured Actor in a Play") at the [72nd Tony Awards](/wiki/72nd_Tony_Awards "72nd Tony Awards").[[70]](#cite_note-70) Cera return to Broadway in October 2018, starring in a third Kenneth Lonergan production, a revival of *[The Waverly Gallery](/wiki/The_Waverly_Gallery "The Waverly Gallery")* at the [John Golden Theatre](/wiki/John_Golden_Theatre "John Golden Theatre") on Broadway. Cera took a supporting role as a painter acting alongside [Elaine May](/wiki/Elaine_May "Elaine May"), [Lucas Hedges](/wiki/Lucas_Hedges "Lucas Hedges"), and [Joan Allen](/wiki/Joan_Allen "Joan Allen").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Cera co-starred in the 2018 film drama *[Gloria Bell](/wiki/Gloria_Bell "Gloria Bell")*, with [Julianne Moore](/wiki/Julianne_Moore "Julianne Moore") as the title character.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Cera returned to his role as George Michael Bluth in the fifth season of *[Arrested Development](/wiki/Arrested_Development_(season_5) "Arrested Development (season 5)")* in 2018.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In 2021, Cera lent his voice to the adult animated film *[Cryptozoo](/wiki/Cryptozoo "Cryptozoo")*.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Cera appeared in the 2022 animated comedy *[Paws of Fury: The Legend of Hank](/wiki/Paws_of_Fury:_The_Legend_of_Hank "Paws of Fury: The Legend of Hank")* about a dog who wishes to become a samurai.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In 2023, he starred opposite [Margot Robbie](/wiki/Margot_Robbie "Margot Robbie") and [Ryan Gosling](/wiki/Ryan_Gosling "Ryan Gosling") in the fantasy comedy film [*Barbie*](/wiki/Barbie_(film) "Barbie (film)") as [Allan](/wiki/Allan_(Barbie) "Allan (Barbie)"). In August 2023, he said in an interview he was working on getting financing to direct his first movie,[[76]](#cite_note-76) naming an adaptation of *[Masters of Atlantis](/wiki/Masters_of_Atlantis "Masters of Atlantis")* by [Charles Portis](/wiki/Charles_Portis "Charles Portis") as one of two possible [directorial debuts](/wiki/Directorial_debut "Directorial debut"), having narrowly beaten out Clark Duke when first securing the adaptation rights.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-PlaylistPortis-78)

In 2025, Cera garnered critical acclaim for his performance as Norwegian entomologist Björn Lund in the [Wes Anderson](/wiki/Wes_Anderson "Wes Anderson") film *[The Phoenician Scheme](/wiki/The_Phoenician_Scheme "The Phoenician Scheme")*, which critic Brian Tallerico deemed "his best work to date".[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Michael_Cera_2011.jpg/250px-Michael_Cera_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Cera_2011.jpg)

Cera performing in 2011

In 2010, Cera contributed mandolin and backing vocals to the [Weezer](/wiki/Weezer "Weezer") song "Hang On" from their album *[Hurley](/wiki/Hurley_(album) "Hurley (album)")*.[[82]](#cite_note-slant-82) Cera has also established himself as the touring bass player in [Mister Heavenly](/wiki/Mister_Heavenly "Mister Heavenly"), an indie rock band originating in the American northwest,[[83]](#cite_note-83) and is a member of the band [The Long Goodbye](/wiki/The_Long_Goodbye_(band) "The Long Goodbye (band)"), along with [Clark Duke](/wiki/Clark_Duke "Clark Duke").[[84]](#cite_note-spin-1-84) Cera also played bass and sang back up during songs in both *Scott Pilgrim vs. the World* and *Nick and Norah's Infinite Playlist*. He released his full-length debut album *[True That](/wiki/True_That "True That")* on August 8, 2014, through his official [Bandcamp](/wiki/Bandcamp "Bandcamp") page.[[85]](#cite_note-p4k-album-85)[[86]](#cite_note-teleg-86) The album features 19 original tracks, a cover of [Roderick Falconer](/wiki/Roderick_Falconer "Roderick Falconer")'s "Play It Again" as well as a cover of [Blaze Foley](/wiki/Blaze_Foley "Blaze Foley")'s "Clay Pigeons."[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In early 2015, Canadian musician [Alden Penner](/wiki/Alden_Penner "Alden Penner") released "Meditate", a track from his upcoming [EP](/wiki/Extended_Play "Extended Play") *Canada in Space*, which features Cera. Penner subsequently announced that the EP would be released on June 29, 2015, on [City Slang](/wiki/City_Slang "City Slang") records, as well as a European tour of the UK, Netherlands, France, and Germany, which featured Cera as both co-headliner and member of Penner's backing band.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) The song "Best I Can" from the film *[Dina](/wiki/Dina_(film) "Dina (film)")*, written and performed by Cera and featuring [Sharon Van Etten](/wiki/Sharon_Van_Etten "Sharon Van Etten"), was nominated for 'Best Song in a Documentary' at the 2017 Critics' Choice Documentary Awards.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Cera&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Cera has been very private about his personal life.[[91]](#cite_note-Fatherhood-91) In 2016, [Aubrey Plaza](/wiki/Aubrey_Plaza "Aubrey Plaza") made public that the pair had dated for about 18 months after filming *[Scott Pilgrim vs. the World](/wiki/Scott_Pilgrim_vs._the_World "Scott Pilgrim vs. the World")* in 2010 and even considered getting married at one point. The two remain friends.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

In March 2022, [Amy Schumer](/wiki/Amy_Schumer "Amy Schumer") accidentally revealed that Cera was a father.[[91]](#cite_note-Fatherhood-91) Later that month, he revealed to *Extra* that the baby was a six-month-old boy with his German wife Nadine.[[93]](#cite_note-93) In a February 2024 appearance on *[The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show_Starring_Jimmy_Fallon "The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon")*, Cera announced that he and his wife had a second son.[[94]](#cite_note-94) In his Criterion Closet Picks video (posted on May 30, 2025), Cera shared that his "older son is three and a half".[[95]](#cite_note-95) As of August 2023, Cera resides in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)
